patterns.factory-method.title: Factory Method
patterns.factory-method.definition: A creational design pattern that provides an interface for creating objects in a superclass, but allows subclasses to alter the type of objects that will be created.
patterns.factory-method.principle: Objects are not created directly using the 'new' operator, but through a call to a special factory method. For this system to work, all returned objects (products) must have a common abstract interface. This allows client code to work with them uniformly, while subclasses can override the factory method to change the class of the created product.
patterns.factory-method.structure: 1. Product — defines a common abstract interface for all produced objects. 2. Concrete Products — specific implementations of this interface. 3. Creator — a base class that declares the factory method, which returns an object of type Product. 4. Concrete Creators — subclasses that override the factory method to create and return a specific product.
patterns.factory-method.usage: The pattern is used when a class doesn't know beforehand the exact types and dependencies of the objects it should work with. It's also used when a class wants its subclasses to specify the objects it creates, or when you need to save system resources by reusing existing objects instead of rebuilding them.
patterns.factory-method.pros: Decouples client code from concrete product classes. Moves object creation code to a single place, simplifying maintenance. Makes it easier to add new products to the program, implementing the Open/Closed Principle.
patterns.factory-method.cons: Can lead to code complexity and large parallel class hierarchies, as you need to create a corresponding creator subclass for each new product class.
patterns.factory-method.related: Abstract Factory (often implemented with factory methods). Template Method (base classes often call factory methods within template methods). Prototype and Builder (architectures often evolve from Factory Method toward these patterns when more flexibility is required).
patterns.abstract-factory.title: Abstract Factory
patterns.abstract-factory.definition: A creational design pattern that lets you produce families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes.
patterns.abstract-factory.principle: The pattern provides an interface for creating all types of products within a family. Instead of creating objects directly, the client calls the factory's methods. This ensures that all created products are compatible with each other, as they are produced by the same concrete factory.
patterns.abstract-factory.structure: 1. Abstract Products — interfaces for a set of distinct but related products. 2. Concrete Products — implementations of those interfaces, grouped by variant. 3. Abstract Factory — an interface that declares a set of methods for creating each of the abstract products. 4. Concrete Factories — implement the creation methods to return specific variants of products.
patterns.abstract-factory.usage: Use the pattern when your code needs to work with various families of related products, but you don't want it to depend on the concrete classes of those products. It’s also useful when a system has a set of Factory Methods that you should consolidate into one Abstract Factory.
patterns.abstract-factory.pros: Ensures that products you get from a factory are compatible with each other. Avoids tight coupling between concrete products and client code. Supports Single Responsibility and Open/Closed principles.
patterns.abstract-factory.cons: The code may become more complicated than it should be, since a lot of new interfaces and classes are introduced along with the pattern. Requires implementing all product types for every new factory variant.
patterns.abstract-factory.related: Factory Method (Abstract Factory is often based on a set of Factory Methods). Prototype (can be used to implement the factory). Builder (can return products as an Abstract Factory).
patterns.active-record.title: Active Record
patterns.adapter.title: Adapter
patterns.bridge.title: Bridge / Handle/Body
patterns.bridge.definition: A structural design pattern that separates an abstraction from its implementation (dividing one or more classes into two separate hierarchies) so that the two can vary independently.
patterns.bridge.principle: The pattern replaces static inheritance with aggregation or composition by carving out one of the dimensions of class development into a separate hierarchy. The Abstraction (the control interface) doesn't perform work on its own but delegates it to an attached object from the Implementation layer. This avoids a combinatorial explosion of subclasses and allows switching the implementation at runtime.
patterns.bridge.structure: 1. Abstraction — defines the control interface and maintains a reference to an implementation object. 2. Refined Abstraction — extends the interface or contains variations of the control logic. 3. Implementor — defines the base abstract interface for all concrete implementations. 4. Concrete Implementor — contains specific platform-dependent code. 5. Client — interacts exclusively with Abstraction objects.
patterns.bridge.usage: Used when you want to avoid a permanent binding between an abstraction and its implementation, when a class needs to be extended in two independent dimensions, or when changes in the implementation shouldn't require recompiling client code. It's also useful for breaking down a monolithic class that combines several different implementations, simplifying its maintenance.
patterns.bridge.pros: Decouples implementation from the interface and hides unnecessary or dangerous details from clients. Increases independent extensibility of the system and allows building platform-independent programs, supporting the Open/Closed Principle.
patterns.bridge.cons: Using the pattern complicates the program code due to the introduction of additional classes and hierarchies.
patterns.bridge.related: Adapter (unlike Bridge, which is designed into the architecture beforehand, Adapter is applied after the fact to connect already existing incompatible classes). Abstract Factory (can be used together to encapsulate the creation of desired abstractions and implementations). Builder (can be implemented based on the Bridge principle, where the director acts as the abstraction and builders as the implementations).
patterns.builder.title: Builder
patterns.builder.definition: A creational design pattern that separates the construction of a complex object from its representation. This allows you to produce complex objects step-by-step and use the same construction process to create different representations.
patterns.builder.principle: The process of creating an object is moved outside its own class and broken down into individual steps which are assigned to special builder objects. The code that calls the construction steps works with builders via a common interface, allowing them to be interchangeable. The order of step calls is usually managed by a Director class, which hides the assembly details from client code.
patterns.builder.structure: 1. Builder — defines an abstract interface for creating parts of a Product. 2. Concrete Builder — constructs parts of the product, defines its representation, and provides a method for retrieving the result. 3. Director — defines the order of construction steps. 4. Product — the complex object being built. 5. Client — creates builder and director objects, links them, and requests the final result.
patterns.builder.usage: Used when the algorithm for creating a complex object should be independent of its parts and how they are assembled. It's ideal for replacing 'telescopic constructors' with a massive number of optional parameters. It's also used when the construction process must allow for different representations of an object, or when you need to assemble complex composite objects like Composite trees step-by-step.
patterns.builder.pros: Provides finer control over the construction process by allowing products to be built step-by-step. Isolates complex assembly code and internal representation details from the main business logic. Allows reusing the same construction code to create various product variants.
patterns.builder.cons: Increases overall code complexity since you need to introduce new classes for builders, products, and the director. Client code often becomes tied to concrete builder classes because the products they produce can vary significantly and might not have a common abstract class.
patterns.builder.related: Abstract Factory (unlike Builder, which returns the product at the final step, Abstract Factory returns products immediately and specializes in creating families of objects). Composite (complex trees are often assembled using Builder). Bridge (Builder can be implemented as a Bridge where Director acts as the abstraction and builders as implementations). Singleton (can be used to create a single instance of the Director or concrete builders).
patterns.chain-of-responsibility.title: Chain of Responsibility
patterns.command.title: Command
patterns.composite.title: Composite
patterns.composite.definition: A structural design pattern that lets you compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Composite lets clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly.
patterns.composite.principle: The pattern suggests treating simple objects (Leaves) and complex objects (Containers) via a common interface. Composite objects delegate request execution to their child components in a recursive chain, then may add something of their own to the result or summarize it. This allows client code to work with the entire tree structure as if it were a single object.
patterns.composite.structure: 1. Component — defines the common interface for both simple and complex components of the structure. 2. Leaf — represents leaf nodes that have no children and perform the primary work. 3. Composite (Container) — contains a set of child elements (both leaves and composites) and delegates method calls to them. 4. Client — manipulates the objects in the composition exclusively through the common abstract interface of the component.
patterns.composite.usage: Used when you need to represent a hierarchy of objects (business logic) as a tree structure (part-whole model). It's also necessary when clients must treat simple and composite objects uniformly, hiding the differences in their internal representation.
patterns.composite.pros: Simplifies client architecture by removing the need for branching functions based on component type (leaf or composite). Makes it easier to add new types of components (both leaves and containers) without requiring changes in client code.
patterns.composite.cons: The pattern tends to create an overly general class design. As a result, it becomes difficult to establish rigid restrictions on which specific types of objects can be part of the composition, as the static type system doesn't allow for it, necessitating runtime checks.
patterns.composite.related: Builder (used for step-by-step creation of Composite trees). Iterator (used to traverse the structure of composite objects). Visitor (applied to perform new operations over the entire tree). Decorator (has a similar recursive nesting structure and is often combined with Composite to override functions of individual tree parts). Flyweight (allows sharing leaf nodes to save memory). Chain of Responsibility (can use parent references in the Composite structure to pass requests up the hierarchy).
